title: Minimizing the Economic Burden of the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act Pending Repeal

flags.justification: Grants 'maximum extent permitted by law' discretion to the HHS Secretary and all agency heads with ACA authorities to waive, defer, grant exemptions from, or delay ACA implementation - extremely broad delegated discretion on the first day of the Administration.

'To the maximum extent permitted by law' and 'all authority and discretion available to them' - no defined standard for what burdens qualify or what waivers should be granted, no reporting or review requirement.
Bundles a waiver/exemption/delay directive, a state-flexibility directive, and a free-market-encouragement directive across multiple sections into a single order.

The terms 'fiscal burden,' 'cost,' 'fee,' 'tax,' 'penalty,' and 'regulatory burden' are given no defined threshold or process, leaving essentially any ACA requirement subject to agency-discretion nonenforcement - an extraordinarily open-ended vague-enforcement structure for a first-day order.

Agencies are directed to 'waive, defer, grant exemptions from, or delay' burdens without any accountability mechanism for insurers or individuals who rely on the waived provisions remaining unenforced, creating a policy-uncertainty incentive problem for regulated parties.
No sunset; explicitly 'pending such repeal,' open-ended until legislative repeal.
No formal oversight-preemption mechanism; Section 5 explicitly requires Administrative Procedure Act compliance for any actual regulatory revision, an accountability-preserving feature.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.

A first-day executive order beginning a policy of non-enforcement discretion toward the ACA pending an anticipated legislative repeal that, as a matter of well-documented subsequent history, was never fully achieved (the ACA remained the governing statute throughout the Trump first term) - a notable parallel to this stream's EO13492 Guantanamo finding (Obama Chunk 1) of a stated policy-reversal goal not matching subsequent political reality.
Single HHS-led directive extended to all agencies with ACA responsibilities, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no formal exemption regime distinct from the broad waiver authority already captured in Flag1/Flag5.

Bundles a high-priority designation process, deadline-setting authority, and a written-explanation-for-delay requirement into a single order.

'High priority' criteria are undefined beyond 'importance to the general welfare, value to the Nation, environmental benefits, and such other factors as the Chairman deems relevant' - an almost entirely discretionary standard.
Not applicable - no clear third-party incentive structure identified distinct from Flag2/4.
No sunset; a standing coordination framework.
No oversight-preemption mechanism; agencies failing to meet deadlines must provide a written explanation to the CEQ Chairman, a modest accountability-preserving feature.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.
A novel infrastructure-review-acceleration order with no direct predecessor EO; distinct from, but conceptually adjacent to, this stream's broader tracked pattern of NEPA-review-streamlining orders.
Single CEQ-led coordination role, agencies retain their own review authority.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Border Security and Immigration Enforcement Improvements

flags.justification: Directs the Secretary of Homeland Security to 'immediately take' wide-ranging steps (wall construction, detention facility construction, hiring 5,000 agents) under broad 'operational control' discretion 'as determined by the Secretary.'

'Operational control, as determined by the Secretary' is self-defining; the Secretary determines both the goal and whether it has been achieved, with periodic reporting to the President but no independent verification standard.

An extraordinarily dense 17-section order bundling physical wall construction, detention facility expansion, asylum officer/immigration judge assignment, expanded expedited removal, termination of 'catch and release,' 287(g) state-local deputization agreements, parole/asylum-provision restrictions, foreign aid reporting on Mexico, biometric tracking, and a transparency-reporting regime - one of the most structurally dense single orders this stream has tracked at any point in the corpus.
'Operational control,' 'all appropriate action,' and 'to the extent permitted by law' qualifiers pervade nearly every substantive directive, leaving vast operational discretion undefined.
Not applicable - no clear perverse incentive structure identified distinct from Flag2/4.
No sunset; a standing enforcement framework.

No oversight-preemption mechanism; multiple congressional and public reporting requirements are built in (Sections 9, 14, 15), an accountability-preserving feature partially offsetting the order's broad discretion.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A landmark, first-week immigration-enforcement order with no direct executive-order predecessor of comparable scope in this corpus; its 'sole and unreviewable discretion' language in Section 11(c) (regarding expedited removal designations) is a notably strong unreviewability clause this stream will watch for recurrence.

Section 12 directs coordination between the Secretary and the Secretary of the Interior over Federal-lands access, and Section 10 layers 287(g) state-local partnerships atop existing Federal immigration-enforcement authority, an acknowledged multi-jurisdictional coordination structure.
Not applicable - no formal exemption regime distinct from the case-by-case discretionary authorities already captured elsewhere.

Bundles enforcement-priority criteria, a civil fines/penalties directive, a hiring surge, sanctuary-jurisdiction grant-conditioning, a Privacy-Act-exclusion directive for non-citizens, a crime-victims office, and a data-transparency regime into a single 18-section order.

No sunset; a standing enforcement framework.

Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

Section 14's directive that agencies 'exclude persons who are not United States citizens or lawful permanent residents from the protections of the Privacy Act regarding personally identifiable information' is a genuinely novel and consequential civil-liberties-narrowing structural provision this stream has not previously tracked at this level of explicitness; combined with the sanctuary-jurisdiction grant-conditioning mechanism (subsequently subject to significant federal litigation, as a matter of well-documented history), this order represents one of the most legally contested first-month actions in the entire corpus.
Clear DHS/DOJ lead roles, though Section 9's joint Attorney General/Secretary discretion over grant-conditioning creates some latent overlap.

The pledge's substantive restrictions (5-year lobbying ban, 2-year cooling-off period, gift ban, etc.) are precisely and numerically defined.

title: Reducing Regulation and Controlling Regulatory Costs

flags.justification: The OMB Director is granted broad discretion to set agency-specific regulatory cost budgets and to approve or deny individual regulations against that budget.

'Unless otherwise required by law or consistent with advice provided in writing by the Director' - the Director's cost-offset guidance and waiver determinations are essentially unreviewable, with no defined methodology mandated in the order itself (left to future OMB guidance).
Bundles a 2-for-1 repeal-offset mandate, a zero-net-cost fiscal-year cap, an annual regulatory cost submission process, and a Unified Regulatory Agenda gating requirement into a single order.

'Regulation' is defined to exclude military/national-security/foreign-affairs and agency-organization/personnel rules (Section 4), and the actual cost-measurement methodology is left entirely to future OMB Director guidance not specified in this order.

A structurally significant perverse-incentive mechanism: mandating that agencies repeal two existing regulations for every one new regulation, regardless of the substantive merits of either the new rule or the ones selected for repeal, creates an incentive to identify low-cost, easily-repealable rules to 'bank' against future high-priority rulemaking needs, independent of whether the repealed rules serve legitimate public-protection functions - a novel finding better characterized as a 'regulatory-bookkeeping' incentive distortion, distinct from the more familiar Zombie-Emergency variant.
No sunset; the fiscal-year-2017 cap is transitional but the underlying 2-for-1/cost-budget structure is stated to continue 'for each fiscal year thereafter.'
No formal oversight-preemption mechanism; APA notice-and-comment requirements for actual rule repeals are preserved (implicit throughout, consistent with Section 2(c)).
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.

Operates alongside the pre-existing EO12866 (this stream: 30.0%, the 1993 Clinton-era regulatory review order) review process without amending or revoking it, layering an entirely new cost-offset budget mechanism atop the existing centralized regulatory-review architecture - a continuity-preserving addition rather than a replacement, distinct from this stream's more commonly tracked amendment/revocation patterns.

Clear OMB Director-led coordination, though the order acknowledges (Section 2(d)) that agency total-incremental-cost allowances must be individually negotiated during the budget process, an implicit coordination point.

Section 4's exclusion of military/national-security/foreign-affairs regulations and agency-organization/personnel rules from the 'regulation' definition, plus the Director's Section 4(c) authority to exempt 'any other category,' constitutes a genuine, Director-discretion-driven exemption architecture.

title: Combating Public Health Emergencies and Strengthening National Security by Ensuring Essential Medicines, Medical Countermeasures, and Critical Inputs Are Made in the United States

flags.justification: All procuring agencies granted broad discretion over domestic-sourcing procurement restrictions, with HHS/DPA-delegated priority-and-allocation authority for essential medicines and Defense granted domestic-restriction authority.

The broad, largely self-certifying 'public interest' waiver standard (Section 2(f)(i)(1)) - an agency head's own written determination suffices to exempt a specific contract from the domestic-sourcing requirements - lacks independent verification standards.

An extraordinarily dense 10-section order bundling a domestic-procurement-preference directive, an e-commerce-platform-compliance-review directive, a supply-chain-vulnerability-identification directive (with FDA regulatory changes), a streamlined-regulatory-requirements directive for Advanced Manufacturing facilities, a DPA Title I priority/allocation delegation (building on EO13603), and extensive multi-part definitions and waiver criteria - one of the most structurally dense public-health-industrial-base orders this stream has tracked, spanning procurement law, trade policy, FDA regulation, and DPA authority simultaneously.

'Public interest,' 'sufficient and reasonably available commercial quantities,' and 'satisfactory quality' throughout leave substantial agency-level interpretive discretion without precisely quantified criteria (aside from the specific 25-percent cost-increase waiver threshold, one of this stream's more precisely-quantified Flag4 findings).

The order's own findings explicitly diagnose a market-failure/supply-chain-vulnerability problem ('it is critical that we reduce our dependence on foreign manufacturers... to ensure sufficient and reliable long-term domestic production... to minimize potential shortages') as the rationale for restructuring federal procurement incentives toward domestic sourcing.
No sunset; a standing procurement-preference framework, though recurring annual reporting is required starting December 2021.

No oversight-preemption mechanism; the mandatory annual written-determination-and-reporting requirement for public-interest waivers (Section 2(h)) is a genuine, notable accountability-preserving feature.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.

Explicitly builds on the DPA delegation authority already established under EO13603 (this stream: 55.0%) and the COVID-19 emergency context tracked extensively since Chunk 6, applying it specifically to a permanent domestic-industrial-base-building program for essential medicines rather than an emergency-only response - a notable structural shift from short-term emergency delegation toward permanent procurement-policy reform.
Clear multi-agency structure (all procuring agencies, plus FDA/OMB/USTR/Defense/EPA coordination for specific sub-tasks), no competing-claims structure.

Section 2(f)'s explicit three-category waiver (public interest, insufficient domestic supply, excessive cost increase) plus Section 8's rule-of-construction preserving State/local emergency procurement and COVID-19-response authority constitutes a genuine, substantial exemption architecture.

title: Fighting the Spread of COVID-19 by Providing Assistance to Renters and Homeowners

flags.justification: HHS/CDC/Treasury/HUD/FHFA granted broad discretion over eviction-moratorium consideration, financial-assistance identification, and foreclosure-prevention-authority review, though much of the order is precatory ('shall consider') rather than self-executing.

'Reasonably necessary to prevent the further spread of COVID-19' and the largely aspirational 'shall consider whether... measures... are reasonably necessary' framing (Section 3(a)) lack independent, objective verification standards for whether any resulting action is actually justified by public-health necessity versus economic-relief motivations.

Bundles a CDC public-health-necessity-consideration directive, a Treasury/HUD funds-identification directive, a HUD landlord/housing-authority-assistance directive, and an FHFA authority-review directive into a single 4-section order spanning public health, housing finance, and federal funding domains.

'Reasonably necessary,' 'as appropriate,' and the underlying, undefined universe of 'available Federal funds' throughout leave substantial multi-agency interpretive discretion without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; the order's directives persist 'during the ongoing COVID-19 national emergency,' formally tied to the underlying declaration.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel COVID-19 housing-relief order with no direct executive-order predecessor, notable for directly precipitating the CDC's subsequent nationwide eviction moratorium (issued days later under the public-health-necessity authority this order directs CDC to 'consider') - though the moratorium itself was issued as an agency rule rather than a further executive order this stream tracks.
Clear HHS/CDC/Treasury/HUD/FHFA multi-agency coordination structure, an acknowledged, modest coordination mechanism given the order's cross-cutting public-health/housing-finance scope.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Targeting Opportunity Zones and Other Distressed Communities for Federal Site Locations

flags.justification: A narrow, technical amendment to two pre-existing Federal-space-management executive orders, redirecting federal facility-siting preference toward qualified opportunity zones and other distressed communities; a compact textual-revision action with no independent structural governance mechanism at issue beyond the amendment itself.
Not applicable - routine technical/administrative order.
Not applicable - single narrow technical action.
Not applicable - no enforcement mechanism at issue.
Not applicable - no incentive structure created.
Not applicable - not a time-limited grant of authority.

A narrow, technical amendment to two pre-existing Federal-space-management executive orders, redirecting federal facility-siting preference toward qualified opportunity zones and other distressed communities; a compact textual-revision action with no independent structural governance mechanism at issue beyond the amendment itself.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive structure.

Amends EO12072 of 1978 (Federal Space Management; not coded by this stream - already coded by other coders in the Carter batch, e.g. claude-church-bells-v1: 20.0%) and EO13006 (this stream: 20.0%, 1996 Clinton's Locating Federal Facilities on Historic Properties in our Nation's Central Cities order) by redirecting federal-space-siting preference from historic central cities toward qualified opportunity zones and other distressed areas - a substantive priority shift layered onto two decades-old procedural frameworks via targeted textual amendment rather than wholesale revocation and replacement.
Not applicable - no competing agency claims.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Lowering Drug Prices by Putting America First

flags.justification: HHS Secretary granted discretion to design and implement a Medicare Part B most-favored-nation pricing test model.
'To the extent consistent with law' and the Secretary's own model-design discretion lack independent verification standards for the model's specific parameters.
Single coherent subject (a Medicare Part B most-favored-nation pricing test model).

'Most-favored-nation price,' while defined with a specific formula (Section 2(b)), still leaves substantial Secretary-level discretion over the specific test model's implementation details, which countries/products are compared, and volume-adjustment methodology.

The order's own findings explicitly diagnose a monopsony-power-underutilization problem: 'in most markets, the largest buyers pay the lowest prices, but this has not been true for prescription drugs... the Federal Government is the largest payer... but it pays more than many smaller buyers' - an explicit, direct incentive/pricing-structure diagnosis embedded in the order's own text.

No sunset; a standing rulemaking-implementation directive, though this order is itself REVOKED just 51 days later by EO13948 (this chunk) - one of the shortest-lived executive orders this stream has tracked in the entire corpus.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.
A novel Medicare drug-pricing order with no direct executive-order predecessor, issued the same day as the EO13937/13938/13939 drug-pricing trio (Chunk 7) though published separately from that group.
Single HHS-led structure, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

notes: Applicable: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,9,10 = 9 (NA: 8,11). raw=1+1+0+1+1+0+0+0+0=4. 4/18=22.22%. NOTE: this order is revoked by EO13948 (this chunk) 51 days later.

title: Lowering Drug Prices by Putting America First

flags.justification: HHS Secretary granted discretion to design and implement most-favored-nation pricing test models for BOTH Medicare Part B and Part D, an expansion of scope over its revoked predecessor (Part B only).

'To the extent consistent with law' and 'to the extent feasible' throughout, plus the Secretary's own model-design discretion, lack independent verification standards for the models' specific parameters.

Bundles a Medicare Part B most-favored-nation pricing-model directive, a NEW Medicare Part D most-favored-nation pricing-model directive, and a revocation of the immediately preceding EO13947 into a single 6-section order.

'Insufficient competition exists' and 'to the extent feasible' for the new Part D model leave substantial Secretary-level discretion over the model's scope and implementation without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.

Repeats and extends EO13947's own monopsony-power-underutilization diagnosis to now cover both Medicare Part B and Part D drug spending - an explicit, direct incentive/pricing-structure diagnosis embedded in the order's own text.
No sunset; a standing rulemaking-implementation directive.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.

Revokes EO13947 (this stream: 22.22%, this chunk, issued just 51 days earlier) and replaces it with an expanded version covering both Medicare Part B and Part D - one of the shortest predecessor-to-successor intervals this stream has tracked in the entire corpus, a rapid same-administration self-correction/expansion rather than a policy reversal.
Single HHS-led structure, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Blocking Property of Certain Persons With Respect to the Conventional Arms Activities of Iran

flags.justification: State/Treasury Secretaries granted broad IEEPA blocking-designation authority over conventional-arms-trade participants with Iran, the fifth escalation this stream has tracked in the post-JCPOA-withdrawal Iran sanctions family.

The multi-category, broadly-worded designation criteria (materially contributes, materially assists, owned/controlled) and the implicit no-prior-notice structure lack independent verification standards.
Single coherent subject (a conventional-arms-trade-specific blocking/entry-suspension structure), not unusually dense relative to this stream's typical IEEPA-order baseline.

'Materially contributes' and 'poses a risk of materially contributing' leave substantial Secretary-level interpretive discretion without objective evidentiary thresholds specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no incentive structure at issue distinct from the underlying emergency this order escalates.
Nominal annual NEA/IEEPA renewal tied to the underlying EO12957 emergency, de facto standing but formally time-bound by statute.

No oversight-preemption mechanism beyond the standard IEEPA no-prior-notice provision already captured in Flag2; Section 9's explicit agricultural/food/medicine/medical-device exemption is a genuine humanitarian-carve-out accountability-preserving feature.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

The fifth escalation this stream has tracked within the post-JCPOA-withdrawal Iran sanctions family (EO13846 Chunk 4; EO13871, EO13876 Chunk 5; EO13902 Chunk 7; this order), explicitly invoked pursuant to the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (CAATSA) in addition to IEEPA - continuing the most escalation-dense single-country sanctions family this stream has tracked outside Venezuela.
Single Treasury/State implementing structure, no competing-claims structure.

Section 9's explicit humanitarian carve-out (agricultural commodities, food, medicine, medical devices) constitutes a genuine, substantial exemption architecture, distinct from the Flag7 finding by being a standalone dedicated section.

title: Combating Race and Sex Stereotyping

flags.justification: Labor Department (OFCCP) and OPM granted broad enforcement/investigation/debarment authority over federal contractors' and agencies' diversity-training content, spanning contracting, grants, and internal agency training simultaneously.

The definitionally broad, contested 'divisive concepts' standard (Section 2(a)'s nine-part list) applied via complaint-driven investigation lacks independent, objective verification standards for what specific training content will trigger enforcement.

An extraordinarily dense 10-section order bundling a detailed nine-part 'divisive concepts' definitional framework, a Uniformed-Services training prohibition, a Federal-contractor contract-clause mandate (with debarment consequences), a Federal-grant certification-conditionality directive, an agency-training-review directive (with adverse-action referral for supervisors), an OPM/OMB training-pre-review requirement, and a Title VII guidance-development directive - one of the most structurally dense, ideologically contested workplace-training-regulation orders this stream has tracked, spanning federal contracting, grants, uniformed services, and internal agency operations simultaneously.

Despite the detailed nine-part enumeration, terms like 'inculcate,' 'promote,' and the underlying question of what constitutes prohibited 'training' versus permitted 'objective' academic discussion (Section 10(b)) leave substantial agency/OFCCP interpretive discretion in individual enforcement determinations.

Section 4's explicit contractor debarment-and-ineligibility consequence for noncompliance, and Section 5's grant-funding certification-conditionality mechanism, constitute a direct financial-incentive-restructuring mechanism designed to induce contractor/grantee compliance, an explicit conditional-funding/contracting incentive structure.
No sunset; a standing contracting/grant/training-compliance framework, though Section 4's contract-clause requirement applies only to contracts entered into 60 days after the order.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure distinct from Flag5.

A novel, highly contested workplace-training-regulation order with no direct executive-order predecessor, though it operationalizes its contractor-enforcement mechanism through EO11246 of 1965 (Equal Employment Opportunity, not itself scored by this stream as it predates the corpus) debarment procedures - among the most politically and legally contested orders this stream has tracked in the entire corpus.
Clear Labor/OFCCP-led enforcement structure with OPM/OMB coordination for agency-training review, an acknowledged, modest multi-agency coordination mechanism.
Not applicable - no exemption regime distinct from the academic-discussion carve-out already captured in Flag4.

title: An America-First Healthcare Plan

flags.justification: Treasury/Labor/HHS/Veterans-Affairs Secretaries directed to 'maintain and build upon existing actions' across an enormous swath of healthcare policy - notably one of the least operationally specific orders this stream has tracked, functioning primarily as a policy-accomplishments summary/consolidation rather than a new grant of authority.

'Shall maintain and build upon existing actions' throughout is an inherently vague continuation directive with no independent verification standard for what specific new actions (if any) are actually required.

Bundles an extensive multi-page findings/accomplishments narrative citing at least a dozen prior executive orders and legislative actions, a healthcare-choice-expansion directive, a healthcare-cost-reduction directive (including a specific surprise-billing legislative-deadline/administrative-fallback mechanism), and a better-care-delivery directive into a single 6-section order.

'Maintain and build upon existing actions' and 'as appropriate' throughout leave substantial Secretary-level interpretive discretion without any new objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself - the order largely restates rather than creates enforceable obligations.
Not applicable - no new incentive structure created distinct from those already established by the numerous prior orders it references.
No sunset; a standing policy-consolidation framework, though the surprise-billing provision has an explicit December 31, 2020 legislative deadline with an administrative fallback.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

An extraordinary consolidation order explicitly citing and building on at least ten prior executive orders this stream has separately tracked and scored (EO13813: 25.0% Chunk 1; EO13941: 27.78% this chunk; EO13877: 31.25% Chunk 5; EO13937: 16.67%, EO13938: 18.75%, EO13939: 27.78%, all Chunk 7; EO13948: 38.89% this chunk; EO13879: 27.78% Chunk 5; EO13861: 25.0% Chunk 5; EO13887: 33.33% Chunk 5; EO13890: 38.89% Chunk 6) - by far the densest single-order cross-reference count this stream has tracked in the entire corpus, functioning as a 'State of Healthcare Policy' retrospective rather than a discrete governance action, issued 41 days before the 2020 Presidential election.
Clear Treasury/Labor/HHS/Veterans-Affairs multi-agency coordination structure, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Protecting Vulnerable Newborn and Infant Children

flags.justification: HHS Secretary granted broad discretion over EMTALA/Rehab-Act enforcement prioritization, investigation, grant-funding prioritization, and complaint-process design.

'As appropriate and consistent with applicable law' and 'as necessary' throughout for the Secretary's own investigation/enforcement/grant-allocation discretion lack independent verification standards.

Bundles an awareness/education directive, an investigation-and-enforcement directive (up to funding termination), a research-grant-prioritization directive, and a training-grant-prioritization directive into a single 4-section order interpreting existing EMTALA/Rehab-Act/Born-Alive-Infants-Protection-Act obligations.

'As appropriate,' 'emergency medical condition,' and the underlying interpretive question of what specific hospital conduct violates existing law leave substantial Secretary-level interpretive and enforcement discretion without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue distinct from Flag2/4.
No sunset; a standing enforcement-interpretation framework.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel, contested enforcement-interpretation order with no direct executive-order predecessor, interpreting three pre-existing statutes (EMTALA, Rehabilitation Act Section 504, Born-Alive Infants Protection Act) rather than creating new statutory authority.
Single HHS-led structure, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Addressing the Threat to the Domestic Supply Chain From Reliance on Critical Minerals From Foreign Adversaries and Supporting the Domestic Mining and Processing Industries

flags.justification: Interior Secretary (with Treasury/Defense/Commerce consultation) granted broad investigative authority under IEEPA plus multiple agencies granted broad discretion across mining/loan-guarantee/permitting-acceleration/research domains.

The multi-agency 'as appropriate' language throughout the extensive policy-coordination sections (Section 2) lacks independent verification standards for whether the resulting mining/processing expansion actually reduces foreign-adversary dependence.

An extraordinarily dense 9-section order bundling a critical-minerals national emergency declaration (with investigation/report requirements), a broad domestic-mining/processing-capacity-building policy directive, a DPA-authority-consideration directive (referencing EO13603), an Energy-Department loan-guarantee-eligibility-clarification directive, a permitting-acceleration directive, a coal-waste/abandoned-mine-reuse directive, and a formal amendment to EO13817 - one of the most structurally dense critical-minerals/industrial-policy orders this stream has tracked, combining a new IEEPA emergency with an amendment to a prior EO and multiple agency-coordination directives simultaneously.

'Avoid, reduce, or sequester,' 'qualifying components,' and the broad 'foreign adversary' designation criteria throughout leave substantial Secretary-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified evidentiary thresholds specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue distinct from Flag2/4.

Nominal annual NEA/IEEPA renewal, de facto standing but formally time-bound by statute; the recurring 180-day threat-update reporting requirement (Section 1(b)) is a genuine, functioning periodic-review mechanism.
No oversight-preemption mechanism; extensive recurring reporting requirements to multiple White House offices throughout are a genuine accountability-preserving feature.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

Formally amends EO13817 (this stream: 31.25%, Chunk 1, A Federal Strategy To Ensure Secure and Reliable Supplies of Critical Minerals) to add a periodic-list-update requirement, while separately invoking (but not amending) the DPA-delegation authority of EO13603 (this stream: 55.0%) for a potential future grant program - a rare instance of an order that both formally amends one predecessor and separately references (without amending) a second, distinguishing genuine amendment from mere invocation.

The order's own text explicitly directs at least six agencies (Interior/Energy/EPA/Army/Agriculture/Commerce) to coordinate permitting acceleration and research efforts (Section 5), an acknowledged, genuine multi-agency coordination structure for a cross-cutting industrial-policy domain.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Saving Lives Through Increased Support for Mental- and Behavioral-Health Needs

flags.justification: A multi-agency Coronavirus Mental Health Working Group established with broad discretion over crisis-service coordination, grant-program review, and vulnerable-population-focused recommendations.

'As appropriate' and the qualitative 'national call to action' framing throughout lack independent verification standards for whether the resulting coordination actually improves mental-health outcomes.

Bundles a Working-Group-establishment section, a four-part national-call-to-action policy directive, a Working-Group-responsibilities section (with a 45-day report deadline), and a grant-funding-encouragement directive into a single 6-section order.
'As appropriate' and 'where appropriate' throughout leave substantial multi-agency interpretive discretion without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing mental-health-coordination framework.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel COVID-19-era mental-health-coordination order with no direct executive-order predecessor, though it references and builds on the pre-existing PREVENTS veteran-suicide-prevention initiative established by EO13861 (this stream: 25.0%, Chunk 5) as a model program without directly amending that order.

The Working Group's own composition (11 named agencies plus HHS/Domestic-Policy dual-Chair) explicitly targets the acknowledged coordination challenge of an 'all-of-government' response to a cross-cutting mental-health crisis spanning defense, justice, agriculture, labor, housing, education, veterans, and small-business domains - a genuine, deliberately-designed broad coordination mechanism.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Establishing the One Trillion Trees Interagency Council

flags.justification: A 20+-member Interagency Council co-chaired by Interior/Agriculture Secretaries granted broad coordination/strategy-development discretion spanning federal tree-growing/restoration/conservation efforts.
The Council's mission/functions (Section 4) are reasonably specifically enumerated across five sub-functions.

Bundles a Council-establishment section (20+ members), an agency-roles-and-responsibilities directive, a mission/functions section (strategy development, limitation-identification, funding-opportunity-identification, State/local/tribal coordination), and a fixed-term termination provision into a single 6-section order.
The Council's membership/functions are reasonably specifically defined with a clear, quantifiable underlying goal (one trillion trees by 2030).
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.

Explicit, functioning sunset: 'The Council shall terminate on December 31, 2030' (Section 5), a genuine, precisely-dated fixed-term structure tied directly to the underlying initiative's own 2030 target date.
No oversight-preemption mechanism; regular progress reporting to Co-Chairs/Vice-Chairs/OMB is required.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

Explicitly builds on and continues the policy established by EO13855 (this stream: 25.0%, Chunk 3, Promoting Active Management of America's Forests, Rangelands, and Other Federal Lands) without amending or revoking it, extending domestic forest-management policy into a new international-commitment-implementation context (the World Economic Forum's One Trillion Trees initiative).
Clear Interior/Agriculture-co-chaired, 20+-agency structure explicitly designed for a novel, exceptionally broad interagency coordination challenge, an acknowledged genuine coordination mechanism.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Modernizing America's Water Resource Management and Water Infrastructure

flags.justification: A multi-agency Water Subcabinet co-chaired by Interior/EPA granted broad discretion over consolidating existing water task forces and developing cross-agency water-infrastructure recommendations.

'As appropriate and consistent with applicable law' and the qualitative 'reduce unnecessary duplication' standard throughout lack independent verification standards for whether the resulting consolidation actually improves water management outcomes.

An extraordinarily dense 9-section order bundling a Water-Subcabinet-establishment directive, an interagency-working-group consolidation directive, a comprehensive four-part water-resource-management report (storage/drought, quality/restoration, systems/flood-control, data/forecasting - each with numerous named sub-initiatives), an integrated-infrastructure-planning directive, and a water-sector-workforce directive into a single order spanning water storage, quality, infrastructure, and workforce domains simultaneously - one of the most structurally dense natural-resources-coordination orders this stream has tracked.

'As appropriate,' 'efficiently and effectively,' and the qualitative coordination standards throughout leave substantial Subcabinet/agency-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing water-policy-coordination framework, though individual reporting tasks have staggered 90/120/150-day deadlines.
No oversight-preemption mechanism; recurring annual status-update reporting to CEQ/OMB/OSTP is required.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel, comprehensive water-policy-coordination order with no direct executive-order predecessor, explicitly directed at consolidating 'hundreds of Federal water-related task forces, working groups, and other formal cross-agency initiatives' into a single Subcabinet structure - a rare, large-scale bureaucratic-consolidation effort this stream has tracked, reducing rather than adding to the federal coordination-body landscape.

The order's own Section 4 explicitly identifies 'reducing inefficiencies and duplication' among 'hundreds' of pre-existing water-related interagency bodies as a central, acknowledged coordination problem the new Water Subcabinet is designed to solve - a genuine, well-documented inter-agency cannibalization/duplication finding directly addressed by the order's own consolidation mandate.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Creating Schedule F in the Excepted Service

flags.justification: Agency heads granted broad, largely unilateral petition authority to reclassify policy-relevant career civil-service positions into a new excepted-service schedule with reduced removal protections - one of the most significant civil-service-structure changes this stream has tracked.

The multi-factor, broadly-worded criteria for Schedule F eligibility (Section 5(c)'s five-part list, including the sweeping 'substantial discretion to determine the manner in which the agency exercises functions' catch-all) combined with the removal of adverse-action procedural protections (chapter 75 exemption) - a structurally significant accountability-reduction mechanism affecting an entire category of career civil servants' job-security protections, a novel and severe application of this stream's Flag2 pattern to personnel law rather than regulatory/enforcement discretion.

An extraordinarily dense 7-section order bundling a new Schedule F excepted-service-category creation (amending Civil Service Rule VI), an adverse-action-procedure exemption (removing chapter 75 protections), a mandatory agency-wide position review (preliminary within 90 days, complete within 210 days, then annual), a five-factor eligibility-criteria list, an OPM petition-approval process, and a collective-bargaining-exclusion-petition directive - one of the most structurally dense and consequential federal-workforce-restructuring orders this stream has tracked in the entire corpus.

'Confidential, policy-determining, policy-making, or policy-advocating character' and 'substantial discretion to determine the manner' throughout leave substantial agency-head interpretive discretion over which positions qualify, without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing civil-service-reclassification framework with ongoing annual review requirements.

No oversight-preemption mechanism; the order's own annual OPM reporting-to-the-President requirement on petitions granted/denied (Section 5(d)) is a modest accountability-preserving feature, though it does not offset the underlying removal of individual employee due-process protections.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel, historically significant civil-service-restructuring order with no direct executive-order predecessor, widely understood at the time as enabling the conversion of potentially tens of thousands of career civil-service positions to at-will-equivalent status - among the most consequential single orders affecting federal-workforce structure that this stream has tracked in the entire corpus.
Single OPM-led approval structure with agency-by-agency petition process, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Establishing the President's Advisory 1776 Commission

flags.justification: Secretary of Education granted authority to establish a 20-member Commission with broad discretion over report content, curriculum-related advisory functions, and agency-coordination for 'patriotic education.'

The qualitative, contested 'patriotic education' and 'accurate, honest, unifying, inspiring, and ennobling' standards throughout (Section 2(c)(iv)) lack independent, objective verification standards for content determinations.

Bundles a Commission-establishment section (20 members, 2-year terms), a multi-part functions section (report production, 250th-anniversary advisory role, Presidential 1776 Award, patriotic-education advisory functions across national parks/monuments, Federal-grant-prioritization advisory functions), a Constitution-Day-compliance-monitoring directive, and a Federal-resource-prioritization directive into a single 5-section order.

'Patriotic education,' 'accurate, honest, unifying, inspiring, and ennobling,' and the underlying contested question of what constitutes appropriate historical curriculum content leave substantial Commission-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue distinct from Flag2/4.
Explicit, functioning sunset: 'The 1776 Commission shall terminate 2 years from the date of this order, unless extended by the President' (Section 2(h)), a genuine fixed-term structure.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel, historically contested education-policy Commission with no direct executive-order predecessor, explicitly framed as a direct ideological counter-response to contemporary historical scholarship the order's own findings section characterizes as 'polemics grounded in poor scholarship' - among the most explicitly ideologically-framed findings sections this stream has tracked in the entire corpus.
Single Education-Department-led structure with State/Defense/Interior/HUD ex-officio coordination, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Addressing the Threat From Securities Investments That Finance Communist Chinese Military Companies

flags.justification: Treasury Secretary (via Defense Department's pre-existing CCMC list) granted broad transaction-prohibition authority over US persons' securities transactions in designated Chinese military-linked companies - a novel extension of IEEPA into public securities-market regulation.

The layered designation process (Defense Department's pre-existing statutory list, plus new Defense/Treasury joint-designation and Treasury-unilateral-designation authority under Section 4(a)(ii)-(iii)) creates a multi-tiered, less-than-fully-transparent designation architecture without independent verification standards for future additions.

Bundles a securities-transaction-prohibition directive (with staggered 60-day/11-month divestment windows), a definitions section incorporating a pre-existing statutory Communist Chinese military company list by reference, and standard evasion/redelegation/reporting provisions into a single order extending sanctions authority into a novel domain (public securities markets) for the first time this stream has tracked.

The layered, evolving CCMC-designation criteria (three separate designation pathways under Section 4(a)) and the underlying question of what companies will be added leave substantial Defense/Treasury interpretive discretion without objective, quantified evidentiary thresholds specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue distinct from the underlying emergency this order creates.

Nominal annual NEA/IEEPA renewal, de facto standing but formally time-bound by statute; the order's own staggered divestment windows (60 days to trade, 365 days to fully divest) are a genuine, functioning phased-implementation timeline.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel securities-market-focused IEEPA emergency with no direct executive-order predecessor, notable as this stream's first-tracked sanctions action targeting publicly-traded securities transactions rather than direct blocking/asset-freezing, extending the existing statutory Communist Chinese military company list (established under a 1999 defense-authorization provision, not itself an executive order this stream tracks) into a novel enforcement mechanism.
Single Treasury-led implementing structure with Defense/State/DNI consultation for designations, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Promoting the Use of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence in the Federal Government

flags.justification: All agencies (excluding Defense/Intelligence Community) directed to adhere to a nine-principle framework, with OMB granted broad discretion over implementing policy-guidance development.

The nine broadly-worded Principles (lawful, purposeful, accurate, safe, understandable, responsible, monitored, transparent, accountable) lack independent, quantified verification standards for compliance, relying on largely self-assessed agency inventories and plans.

Bundles a nine-principle framework, an OMB implementation-guidance-roadmap directive, an agency AI-use-case-inventory directive (with public-disclosure requirement), an interagency-coordination directive, an AI-implementation-expertise/talent directive (Presidential Innovation Fellows AI track), and detailed scope/exclusion definitions into a single 10-section order.

'Appropriate,' 'sufficiently understandable,' and 'significantly outweigh the risks' throughout the nine Principles leave substantial agency-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified compliance thresholds specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing AI-governance framework, though individual implementation tasks have staggered 30/45/60/90/180-day deadlines.

title: Protecting Americans From Overcriminalization Through Regulatory Reform

flags.justification: All agencies directed to adopt new mens-rea-disclosure and strict-liability-justification requirements for regulatory rulemaking, plus new DOJ-referral-consideration guidance, restructuring the internal criminal-enforcement-referral process government-wide.

The order's own accountability rationale is explicit (providing notice of criminal liability, focusing enforcement on culpable actors), and its strict-liability-justification requirement (Section 4(c)) mandates written, published rationale - a genuine transparency/accountability-strengthening measure similar in structure to EO13979 (this chunk).

Bundles a policy-principles section (mens rea disclosure, strict-liability disfavored, culpability focus), a regulatory-transparency requirement (criminal-consequence statements in all NPRMs/final rules), a strict-liability-justification-submission requirement, and a DOJ-referral-consideration-guidance directive into a single 6-section order restructuring regulatory-criminal-enforcement procedures government-wide.

The underlying 'mens rea' analysis and the qualitative referral-consideration factors (Section 5(a)) leave substantial agency-level interpretive discretion in individual enforcement-referral decisions without objective, quantified criteria specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing regulatory-criminal-enforcement-transparency framework, though the referral-guidance-publication task has a 45-day deadline.

No oversight-preemption mechanism; the order's own criminal-consequence-disclosure and strict-liability-justification requirements are genuine, notable transparency/accountability-strengthening features.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel regulatory-criminal-enforcement-transparency order with no direct executive-order predecessor, notable for an unusually extensive six-category exclusion list (Section 6(c)) carving out foreign/military affairs, DOJ criminal/civil enforcement, IP-infringement, strict-liability-misdemeanor plea agreements, employee-misconduct investigations, and a broad agency-head national-security catch-all.
Clear OIRA-coordinated, all-agency structure with DOJ consultation, no competing-claims structure.
Section 6(c)'s explicit six-category exclusion list constitutes a genuine, substantial exemption architecture, notably broader than this stream's typical Flag11 baseline.

title: Protecting the United States From Certain Unmanned Aircraft Systems

flags.justification: All agencies granted broad review/reporting discretion over covered-UAS procurement-cessation authority, plus DNI/OSTP/FAA granted assessment and rulemaking authority.

'Consistent with applicable law' and the underlying review-then-report structure (rather than an immediate mandatory prohibition) lack independent verification standards for whether agencies will actually cease procurement or merely report on their authority to do so.

Bundles a procurement-authority-review directive, a current-UAS-fleet-inventory/risk-assessment directive, a critical-infrastructure-airspace-restriction rulemaking directive (statutorily mandated), and a budget-prioritization directive into a single 7-section order spanning federal procurement, national-security risk-assessment, and aviation-regulation domains.

The multi-part 'covered UAS' definition (Section 6(b)'s five-category test) and 'adversary country' catch-all (any nation 'engaging in long-term patterns... adverse to... security') leave substantial Secretary-of-Commerce-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified evidentiary thresholds for the catch-all category.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing UAS-procurement-security framework.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel UAS-national-security order with no direct executive-order predecessor, though its 'adversary country'/critical-component designation architecture closely parallels this stream's earlier EO13920 (Bulk-Power System, Chunk 7, 43.75%) and EO13913 (Telecommunications, Chunk 6, 31.25%) supply-chain-security template, extending the same structural pattern to a fourth technology domain (drones) within roughly eight months.
Clear multi-agency structure (DNI/OSTP/FAA/OMB coordination for distinct sub-tasks), no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Care of Veterans With Service in Uzbekistan

flags.justification: VA/Defense Secretaries granted discretion over combat-theater designation consideration and a mandatory toxic-exposure epidemiological study.
The study's required elements (Section 2(a)-(c)) are reasonably specifically enumerated (conditions assessment, epidemiological study, causal-link assessment).
Single coherent subject (a narrow veteran-benefits/toxic-exposure-study order for a specific base and date range).
The order's substantive content (specific dates, specific location, specific study elements) is precisely and narrowly defined, an unusually well-anchored, non-vague single-purpose order.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.
No sunset; a standing study-and-consideration directive, though the study itself has a 365-day completion deadline.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

A novel, narrow veteran-health order with no direct executive-order predecessor, addressing a specific toxic-exposure concern (Karshi-Khanabad Air Base, Uzbekistan, 2001-2005) that would later become part of the broader toxic-exposure/burn-pit legislative debate culminating in the 2022 PACT Act (outside this stream's corpus scope).
Single VA/Defense coordination structure, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Revocation of Executive Order 13770

flags.justification: A single-provision revocation of this administration's own January 2017 ethics-pledge order, effective at noon on Inauguration Day; a compact revocation action with no independent structural governance mechanism at issue beyond the revocation itself and its effective-date mechanics.
Not applicable - routine technical/administrative order.
Not applicable - single narrow technical action.
Not applicable - no enforcement mechanism at issue.
Not applicable - no incentive structure created.
Not applicable - not a time-limited grant of authority.

A single-provision revocation of this administration's own January 2017 ethics-pledge order, effective at noon on Inauguration Day; a compact revocation action with no independent structural governance mechanism at issue beyond the revocation itself and its effective-date mechanics.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive structure.

Revokes EO13770 (this stream: 27.78%, Chunk 1, this administration's own January 2017 'Ethics Commitments by Executive Branch Appointees' order) effective at noon on January 20, 2021 - releasing outgoing appointees from the lobbying/foreign-agent restrictions the same administration had imposed on itself four years earlier, timed to take effect at the exact moment of transition to release its own outgoing personnel from those commitments before leaving office, a notable self-releasing bookend to this stream's Chunk 1 finding regarding EO13770's original ethics-pledge architecture.
Not applicable - no competing agency claims.
Not applicable - no exemption regime.

title: Taking Additional Steps To Address the National Emergency With Respect to Significant Malicious Cyber-Enabled Activities

flags.justification: Commerce Secretary granted broad rulemaking authority over identity-verification standards, special-measures restrictions on foreign jurisdictions/persons, and exemption authority for US IaaS (cloud infrastructure) providers - a novel extension of sanctions-adjacent regulatory authority into cloud-computing infrastructure for the first time this stream has tracked.

The multi-factor, broadly-worded 'reasonable grounds' special-measures-triggering standard (Section 2(a)-(c)) and the Secretary's own exemption-authority discretion (Section 1(c)) lack independent, objective verification standards for designation/exemption determinations.

An extraordinarily dense 7-section order bundling an identity-verification-rulemaking directive (with detailed required-recordkeeping elements), a special-measures rulemaking directive (jurisdiction-wide and person-specific prohibitions), an industry-cooperation-recommendations directive, a funding-requirements directive, extensive multi-part definitions, and a formal amendment to EO13694's reporting-authorization provision - one of the most structurally dense cybersecurity/critical-infrastructure orders this stream has tracked, extending sanctions-emergency-adjacent regulatory authority into a novel domain (cloud infrastructure services) for the first time.

'Reasonable grounds,' 'significant number,' and the multi-factor foreign-jurisdiction/foreign-person designation criteria throughout leave substantial Secretary-level interpretive discretion without objective, quantified evidentiary thresholds specified in the order itself.
Not applicable - no clear incentive structure at issue.

No sunset; a standing IaaS-security-regulatory framework, tied to the underlying EO13694 national emergency, though special-measures implementation is delayed 180 days after the identity-verification regulations' issuance.
No oversight-preemption mechanism.
Not applicable - no third-party incentive-gap structure.

Formally amends EO13694 (this stream: 50.0%, as amended by EO13757: 44.44%, Obama's landmark 2015 cyber-sanctions order this stream has repeatedly tracked) by revising its reporting-authorization provision to add Commerce-Secretary consultation, while separately extending its underlying national emergency into an entirely new regulatory domain (cloud-infrastructure identity verification) not previously addressed by that order or its 2016 amendment - the final executive order of the Trump 1st Term, issued the day before the transition, extending one of this stream's most consequential and longest-running tracked sanctions frameworks into its third distinct regulatory application.
Single Commerce-Secretary-led structure with State/Treasury/Defense/AG/DHS/DNI consultation for special-measures designations, no competing-claims structure.
Not applicable - no exemption regime distinct from the Secretary's own exemption-authority already captured in Flag1/2.
